sentences.text: We've got the entire group here, so I'm gonna let them in, we can… Get the conversation going.
Anyone else from our side yet?
Nope.
Maybe it'll just be me and you.
Alright, here we go.
Hi, everyone!
Hey, hey guys, how you doing?
Hey!
Hey.
Hello.
How are you?
Hey, Dale.
Bye!
Alright, so I think from our side, looks like everybody should be on, so I think we just… just jump right in if, if that works for you all.
Yeah, absolutely, Adam, and Megan is joining, who you've met with before.
many times.

Alex Slope, Marcia, will hopefully be joining, as well, so… I know we've got a large group, expensive meeting on both sides, so we'll get through introductions, and then, I think, Adam, if you can maybe guide us a little bit as to where he wants us to go today, and maybe Corey will ask more about that, that'd be great.
I'll start, though, while we wait for Alec.
I'm Matthew Schneck, been Senior Director here with ApartmentIQ, I roll up to Corey.
I've been working with, UDR as the main point of contact for, just around 4 years.
So, I'll kick it over to Corey.
I work with Matthew, so I lead the sales team here at Rentable and ApartmentIQ, excited to take the group through some new features today.
And, Megan, if you wanna… go ahead?
Morning, everyone.
I'm Megan Weil, VP of Product, worked very closely with Alec in building up our new product you're going to see, worked on the market survey, and now focused on our revenue management offering.
So, I'm excited to introduce you to a new face, Trent, who is on the product team as well.
Hi everyone, my name's Trent Riddle.
I'm fairly new to the organization, so still getting my feet underneath me a little bit, but I'll be working on the Explore product that we're demoing today, so excited to meet you.
And Adam, obviously, we see the names on the invite, but maybe broadly, if you can kind of give us a sense of who's joined and kind of the functional roles, I think that would be helpful.

Yeah, definitely, I'll just give a quick, … overview of the different, departments that are on, and anybody can chime in if they want to introduce themselves personally, but we've got folks on the transaction side, on, we've got folks on the development side on, the finance group, and really just wanted to give you know, our side of the house, some visibility into… to the Explore tool, and … You know, some of them have seen it before.

Some have not seen it at all, and I know you guys have enhanced it since, since many have looked at it, so just interested to kind of have everybody on our side take a look at it, and then I'll kind of follow up with them later and figure out, you know, is this something that would aid them in their day-to-day?
And then we can continue conversations internally with that info.

Perfect, and since you're off mute, Adam, I'll address you, but as you indicated, whoever would like to jump in, please please feel free, but obviously, some familiar names in the meeting today, but some new ones as well.
Just more broadly, I'm curious, like, the knowledge or experience within the group relative to just our core offering, the way we're working with you currently.
I guess I'm wondering if a brief introduction to ApartmentIQ would be helpful for at least some members of the group, but….
I think so.
Yeah, maybe just a really, … you know, elevator pitch on DepartmentIQ, and then, drilling into the Explore would be good.
You know, these folks probably, aren't… are not using that market survey tool as often.

I mean, maybe some, but, you know, mostly, I think, in the underwriting and the analysis, side is kind of where they're… they're, their head is, but I'll let them chime in and speak for themselves, so feel free to, … To correct anything I've said, folks.
Boop.
Well, perfect.
If there's no additional input, I think provide a brief overview.
I think as we're demoing Explore today, very easy to just pop over to the Market Survey product and share that with the group.
And Alex joined.
So, Alex, you've met Adam and Dale, and maybe a few others in the room, but Alex, our CEO and co-founder, so….
Nice to see you all.
Meet you all.
As always, Thanks for having me.
And Adam, I guess relative to Explore, we'll focus on that today, as you just shared.
… We know you're using Axio and CoStar for various reasons.
you know, within Explore, I guess if I kind of broke it down, we could… we can speak to and display our… market and sub-market performance trends.
We can kind of take the group through how we're looking at forecasting as well.
There's, you know, market and sub-market demographic data, new supply data, more broadly, just kind of speaking to how we can help within the underwriting process and bring more efficiency.
You can say all of it, but how would you maybe weight the discussion today as you kind of think about the different components of our platform?

If, if you're asking me, I think anything that, is gonna help, folks understand, you know, markets that, or properties that we, that we, you know, we may want to buy or look into in the future, areas we may want to build in in the future.
You know, essentially anything that I think would assist the underwriting process, would really, really be valuable.
Perfect, and… for whoever would like to, respond, I'm curious what's most efficient, or most inefficient or time-consuming within that.
Your workflow today.
Love to kind of focus on that and see how we could help.
I can jump in here.
I'm on the transactions team.
This is Olivia.
I would say just pulling rent comps and understanding rent subject properties.
Thanks, Olivia.
And is that… is that… If we break that down, … Deciding what the comps might be.
Right.
Okay.
Because currently we'll go through Axio and try and find, like, similar vintage, or the closest property in proximity, and it just takes time to dial in our final five.
And would the ideal experience, it's a leading question, be maybe a system that provides the appropriate comps for you based on the key attributes of the assets?
Yes.
Okay.
Nice weekend.
Believe it or not, we can help.
So, we'll show you how you can create a market survey for any property you have your eyes on in literally 3 clicks.
Within that process, our algorithm will provide the best comps that we think you should compare yourselves to, so….
This is, this is Rob.
Speaking, can you guys hear me?
Yep, good.
Yep.

Hey guys, so one… one thing that I'd be curious to know, and you guys will probably touch on it, but just how… when you're determining the pricing by unit type, the survey methodology would be helpful to understand.
I've just seen… you know, in some cases, between Axio and CoStar, differences of… to the tune of $1,000 for the average rent at one property.
It's typically more of, like, a higher-end type luxury community that maybe is in Lisa.

But it's really hard sometimes, and I work on the development side, but the new projects in Lisa tend to be fairly good comps when they're in close proximity, but understanding kind of how… you guys pinpoint, you know, what the… I know it's hard when a project's in lease up, but what really are those rents?

When I see a huge spread like that between data sources, I'll tend to just go to the website and call the property, just to make sure that, you know, which one is, … more correct, but I… it'd be great to know, kind of, how you guys determine that as well.
And how do you feel when… do you then use the… the rent that you see on the website, or how do you… how do you feel about, that value?
Yeah, I mean, that, … tends to be… yeah, I'll lean more towards the website runs.
It's just when we're pulling in a large comp set.
With, you know, sometimes as many as 10 different comps, it's not the most efficient use of time to go to The website and… search all the available floor plans for a property that's stabilized.
A lot of times, there aren't even that many rents available, so it's less helpful, but a project in Lisa, you could have quite a bit of data available.
Yup.
And Rob, specifically on that, the price point, are you looking… are you thinking about, like, a min price point for a particular bed point count?
Is it a median price point?
Where are you seeing the variance?
It's a good question, … It can… it's all over the place.
And again, this isn't that frequent.
But when it… when I do see it, it's pretty alarming, because you're kind of like, okay, which… which one's right?
And we just want to ju… as we're doing our rent comp surveys, we just want to get as close to the pen as possible.

And Rob, I work on Rob's team as well, this is Samantha, development, but I think one of the things that moves the needle as well, we're typically looking at, the average of… by bedroom type, and so, depending on how the unit mix is divided.
Sometimes we'll be looking at high-rises, and so if there's, like, an equal distribution of penthouse units or something like that, that can obviously move the average rent pretty significantly.
So that's one place that I tend to notice discrepancies between.
platforms.
Can absolutely cover that.
That's helpful, and I think I'll step back, just based on what we discussed 5 minutes ago, Adam.
I think, and it speaks to your initial question, Rob, just broadly.
just an overview of who we are and the product you're about to see.

So, we've been, as Matthew indicated, we've been working with UDR for about 4 years, so I think the initial request was, we'd like to automate our market survey process, we'd like to do so using 100% publicly available data, and that's exactly why we exist.
So, we've been storing publicly available rents for 5 years at this point.

Every data point we're going to show you today is updated every single day, so it's the most up-to-date, accurate data in the industry, and everything can be traced all the way down to the unit level.
… We'll show you gross rents, net rents, concessions, everything in between.
We've become quite popular, so we love the partnership that we have with you all.
Dale and Adam have been great in giving us feedback throughout the partnership.
We are currently working with 7 of the top 10 operators in the industry and 33 of the top 50.
So, they've come to us to automate their market survey process, but now, investment teams, development teams, acquisition teams are coming to us and saying.
we love your data.
We'd love your data to be, you know, our primary source of truth internally.
We're using something like a RealPage product or a CoStar product, and the data's just out of date and lagging.
So, we're doing exactly what Rob described.
We're going on property websites to find today's rents, so… That's what we've built.
We've kind of combined this… Daily automated market survey where we're tracking 35 million units every single night.
With some of the core functionality that you all will require as you're looking to build or buy, etc.
Any… any just high-level questions on… on… and I guess I should also address, Rob, are… we generally use the property website as well.
So, ….
Yeah, hard to beat that.
You got it.
Like, it's not easy, just candidly.
There are some less sophisticated operators in the industry that'll use, like, Apartments.
com data and ILS data, but we've found that the property website is just significantly more accurate than any sort of network data points.
Availability on property websites is where we find the most accurate source, and if availability is wrong.
Everything else will be off.
So, we're tracking over a million property websites every single night.
One additional nuance there, we're looking at multiple, sources on a given website.

So, we'll look at the online leasing system, where the applications get processed, but we'll also look at things like the sitemap, if there's a chatbot, we'll look… we'll get availability from that, and we're gonna look and see where the best availability is.

The way that we search the websites, as well, is a bit more nuanced, so… often, properties will try to enforce a floor plan cap, you know, show no more than 5 units per floor plan, 10 units per floor plan to not show full availability.

the way that you actually search a website, the way our crawlers work, is they'll change things like move-in dates, amenities, we'll look for different units across different sources to ensure that we're unearthing as much availability as we possibly can.
And then the system will also flag if it is just one of those properties where we know we aren't seeing 100% of what they've got.
Okay, thanks.
Okay, we'll, we'll show you how you can more easily identify comps.
We'll take you through how you no longer should have to manually check a property website, if you're looking at our tool.
How about market and sub-market data?
What challenges are you facing there when you're comparing multiple markets or sub-markets?
What could you share there?
If anything.
Well, I… I mean, speaking kind of from the development side, I don't… I don't know… there's any unique differences in that sense.
Like, the process of completing the rent comp survey is still the same, specific to that sub-market and market.
And… Vetting, kind of, what… what are the appropriate comps, and then digging into those.
Okay, I wasn't sure if there were just challenges in analyzing trends more broadly at a higher level, but it sounds like maybe not necessarily.

And I think… I don't know, Alec, it might be helpful just to… to show the group what we're talking about, so maybe we can… … Give a peek into the product, and please ask questions throughout, and hopefully.
Yeah, hopefully you like what you see.

Great, so let's… I'm actually going to start and just give you all a little background on what the comp sets that we generate look like first, because we do have acquisitions, new business teams that use just the ability to create comps as a core use case on its own today, and then we'll talk a little bit more about markets and sub-markets as we get into that.
Please feel free to stop me with any questions you've got, or if you want to just put it in the chat, do that.
If my team knows, I'm like, I'm like Ron Burgundy with the teleprompter, if you put it in the chat, I will respond to it, and be probably distracted by it for at least a minute.
So, please feel free to do that.
And we'll go from there.
So, let me pull this up really quick.
So, Corey mentioned that, we've been working with UDR for 4 years now.
And mostly the way we work with you all is we send you a data feed of every unit available at every property, every day.
And so that's the core of our data.

So the first thing you have to understand about ApartmentIQ is we go to property management websites, we capture the exact unit that's available across the entire property, we log that, and we've been doing it since October of 2020.
So, along the sidebar here, you've got my properties, so if you all were… if you're all interested in looking at the market surveys that we've created for your properties today, they would live here.
And then, before I get into the sub-market and market data, this research tab is what's most popular and common with folks.
And so, what this enables you to do is come in, create a new report.
And you can type in an address.
For any property in the country.
Click that address, and it's going to create a new market survey for that property.
And so this is the… this is the crux of what ApartmentIQ has been for.
Corey mentioned our core use case is automating market surveys, and so as you're building out rent comps for your underwriting models, this is a core use case along those.
I'll take you through just a few high-level aspects of this data.
So, it, as you can see, it automatically pulled the 10 most relevant comps for this property, so here's the subject property, and here are the relevant comps.
The algorithm that we're using is looking at about 12 different factors, but it's going to look at everything that you would expect it to look at.
Distance, vintage, size, stories, price, dollar per square foot.
this system is not only going to look at asking rents, but it's also going to pull in net effective rents as well.
So, as you're looking at this market survey, you can see the subject property here, and this is the property rent.
For the subject property, but you've also got a net effective rent, and you can actually see what that concession percentage is, and what the details are.
And the way that we're gathering this data is directly from the property management websites.
We go to the website, nearly always the best source of concession data is on that property website, and we will grab whatever concession flyer there is on that website.
We run it through a pre-trained large language model, which then parses out what does the concession apply to, which units, which floor plans.
And it's going to take whatever information is in that concession, flag and apply it.
And this will also look all the way down to the unit level for their online leasing to see if there's specific concessions at specific unit levels.
For any of these concessions, we also have a history of the concessions that have happened at that property.
So in this case, what you're seeing is a modal that shows the subject property, 1200 Broadway, on a concession value basis, but we can do it as a percentage of rent here.
What is their percentage of rent concession, which this property is pretty aggressively marketing concession right now.
How does that compare to the comp median?
How has that changed over time?
You can always come back, change the timeframe, what the interval is, and then below you can see the actual concessions.
The start date, end date, what the details were, and then which beds were affected by those, and how long that concession was live for.
So you are going to see net effective rent, and so these are the initial, piece of information.
You've also got this comp average, which you can always compare, your property to.

As we drill down here, our system is going to do… we're using publicly available data only, but we can, based on the availability on a property website, we can infer a lot about what's happening at that property.
And so, we use a fairly sophisticated set of algorithms to learn and project what the lease percentage of a property is, what the exposure percentage of a property is.
We also do have occupancy as well.

And then we'll also be able to see leases signed by this property at whatever time period you want, just to give you a sense of What this looks like, so we can go down to the level of seeing what leases were signed in this property in the last 7 days, and you can see the exact units, the floor plan, square footage.
When it was set to be available, what that rent, the ineffective rent, and the concession were associated with that unit.
Yeah, quick question on that, right there.
So, is that…
How do you know that that's what the signed rent was?
We don't know, no.
We know that the unit came off the website, and that's what the price was, and that's what the net effective rent was.
We've done a fair amount of comparisons against true rent rolls.
And generally what we've found is you're within a point or two of the actual price, and it varies a little bit just depending on how many back pocket types of concessions you're gonna see.
Where do you get rent rolls from?
We've had customers that have given it to us so that we can just QA the data.
Okay.
We've also got a reconstructed rent roll that I'll show you, but yeah, that's based off of the actual rent rolls themselves.

So you can see leasing velocity, you can also see things like what the cancel percentage is for a given property, so what percentage of their… of units that are getting applied to, so you can see applications here, are actually turning into leases.

All of this data, so we're looking at today's data here, but all of this data you can change the timeframe from, so a lot of our acquisitions teams, like to look at a trailing 30-day basis for this data, so you can easily do that as well, going back as far as you'd like.
Sorry, one more for me.
Up there where it shows rent, that, at the property level.
Yes.
What does that represent?
That is a, … I think I'm gonna get this right, but Megan, jump in if I get this wrong.
That is a weighted rent average based on the availability of the property.
So that… Just what's available.
That's what the average is.
What was available in that time period, yes.
We also have an in-place rent.
Based on all the units we've ever seen at that property, that I'll show you a little later, if that's what you're, if that's what you're interested in.
Okay.
And then, sorry, you had said that In-place rent, which, … we just looked at over leases in the last 7 days.
You said that that… Is usually within a couple of dollars what the advertised rent was versus The rent roll, when you went back and looked at the data that you could get?
It's typically within 1-3%.
1-3%, got it.
Yeah.
Okay, thanks.
Yep.
I should note as well, with all of these comp sets, you can either… you can always easily add a comp here, so if there's a comp you know you want to include, you can quickly add it there.
You can do that here as well.
And then if you want to discover comps.
or add or remove them, you can always come up to this.
I just clicked to edit comps.
You can easily come in and see, the comps that we've rated.
Here's the comp score associated with each of them, along with some of the factors going into that comp score algorithm.
And then as you scroll down, either from the map here, you can look at other properties that you may want to look at.
You can also filter which properties are going to show up on the map or in this similar properties list.

So you can filter by year built, total units, stories, we have a BTR filter, we built recently the… what we think is the only truly definitive database of BTR properties in the country, class, and amenities as well.

So you can really start to filter down and get a sense of what comps you want to actually include in your comp set or not, though the system's going to make recommendations that, generally speaking, our customers really love.
How do you… how do you determine the comp score?
Yeah, it's, … It's not a weighted average, it's an algorithm that factors in what you're seeing here are the first, call it, 5 or 6 factors that are going into the algorithm.
So distance is the first and most important factor.
And this is not just a standard linear distance, factor, it's all… in a tighter market, it's gonna look at, it's gonna factor in, you know, 0.
1 miles is gonna mean a lot more than in a rural market, where 1 mile might… you still might see a comp.
So there's a nonlinear curve, in the way that we relate to distance.
Year built, renovated or not, total units, stories, price points, both asking rents and installed rents, property class, property type.
… just about everything that you would want to see.

And these things are weighted, by the system dynamically, and the way that we built out our comp… our truth set, like the way that we actually test if our comp scores are any good, is we've got about 5 million units using ApartmentIQ every day, and they pull in their market surveys.

And so they're putting a finalized market survey group together, and so when we update our algorithm, we look to see how closely our comp scores are aligning to what customers, basically across the industry are actually considering comps, and that's how we tune it.
Got it.
And so, for a… for a new development.
If… if we were to put in the location, number of units, average unit size.
estimated year built.
Is that possible?
And then it would just kind of generate, contours based on those metrics.
It is, yep.
So, when I created this first market survey for research, there's also, down here, this new development.
You can create a custom property.
So you can enter the address, year built, total units, and then, across the unit types if you'd like.
We're also going to be adding amenities to this, as well.
And so, you can do this, and that will automatically generate comps for that property.
Got it.
You can also, if I go back here really quick.

You can also use value-add comps as well, so if you're thinking about renovating a property, or if that's part of the analysis of this, it will functionally adjust That per unit cost, depending on the market and what the renovation premiums are on the market.
It's typically about $100 to $150 per unit to see what that step-up comp set would be.
Jumping back into this just really quick, want to make sure I show you all everything else we've got here.
So, pricing, you can always come in.
Here's studio, one beds, and you can decide whether you want to pick the lowest rent for these, an average rent across these, or an absolute minimum.
That we've ever seen in this time period.

You can also change the grouping here, so if you want to change and just see by metric, so you're just looking at rent, rent per square foot, net effective rent, all of that's available here, and this is all gonna come from the property website at a unit level.
It's a good way to gut check what this looks like and how closely these properties are related.
You can also see leasing patterns across these properties, and as you scroll down, you're also going to see the fees and deposits offered by each comp.
This is coming directly from their website, obviously becoming more and more of a factor in the market today.
Amenities as well, as you scroll down.
So, in this case, we're looking at in-unit amenities.
… You can also see community amenities.
And while we're talking about amenities, I should also point out, so I'm just clicking over to this Units tab, and so this is every unit that we're tracking within this comp set.
And for a lot of these properties, actually, they will… about 30% of properties will actually show unit-specific amenities.
… So, you can see for this particular comp, here's all the units we've seen, and this is over the last 30 days.

And you can see exactly what the amenities are by unit to start to get a sense of exactly what differences there are, what's in the units themselves, and so we're getting this directly from the online leasing portion of the website.
You look at, like, in a high-rise building, if you… a unit, say, on level 20 versus 10, Like, quantifying the same unit plan, what that view premium is?
We haven't broke, essentially, like, backed into that, but it's a very common request from, especially from asset managers, so we're working on it right now.
You can always see the floor plan name here, and usually the unit number will tell you what floor it's on.
But actually doing that math is something that we're… we're doing, as it… as… as we speak.
You could also get a sense of, what the best lease length they're offering on these leases are.
You could always filter down to see available units versus actually what got leased.
By comp.
And you can filter to whatever comps you want.
So you could just look at one property if you'd like.
You can filter, across all these data sets by beds, baths, size, rent.
When those units are available, the year built renovation, date of the property.
You can also filter out, if you'd like, you can filter out or in penthouse units, renovated units, affordable units versus the conventional classic units.
And the way we're tagging these right now is we're looking at the naming conventions at the unit level and the floor plan level.
And so most property websites will identify what their renovated floor plans are, what their renovated units are, at some level.
And so we're basing it off of that.
Large language models are basically looking through that information that we grab, and identifying there.

We are also, right now, working on, for units that aren't labeled, but we think are likely renovated, or affordable units, you can infer this just based on variances, with the same floor plan, but different price points.
And so we're working that right now.
Key there is just making sure you don't get a ton of false positives, and so that's, that's being worked on right now, and I think that'll increase the coverage as well on the renovated side.
What about, like, balconies versus no balconies?
Yeah, so you can, right now, … you would have to look at the unit-specific amenities.
Right now, there's not a filter at a unit level that would enable you to do that.
What's the, what's the… Core use case, like, what are you trying to solve for with that particular amenity filter?
Sure.
Yeah, so I think it's more, … From a development perspective, how… what percentage of the unit should have balconies?
And so if there's a… call it a $50 premium, as a trend you see for a… a larger balcony, it may… that marginal return may be worth, you know, adding additional balconies to the project.
Yep.

Yeah, so we see, we see what's on their website, and then we also have access to all the images associated with these properties, because, we actually have an ILS as well, so we have access to all those images, and I'll show you, they actually make their way into our sub-market and market product.
But we also are scanning those images, looking for that.
So we can tag things that way as well, and that's just a simple filter that we could easily add for you to make sure that that works.
Go ahead.
Okay, so this is the core comp product, and … These are always exportable, so you can take this market survey, or the unit data.
And you can click and turn that into a CSV or Excel report, whenever you'd like, work with it.
You can see the CSV export right here.
That Excel export is pretty robust, so it's going to include more than just this market survey.
It's gonna include a ton more information that you can use to underwrite against these properties.
And the last thing that I should… I should show you before we move on to the sub-market market, well, I guess there's two more things I should show you briefly here.
We'll break this down by floor plans as well.

So, you could come in and either search by, you know, number of beds across all the comps to see how that price point changes, what current availability is, and you could customize the timeframe here and see what those trends are.
So if you want to get a sense, for instance, of what that trend is.
On… on rent prices, on a bed-type basis, or even if you'd like to, you can get down to a floor plan level as well.
And start to see what… how many leases did they sign, what's that lease rent per unit, per square foot, and how does that compare to what's available on the market today?
Note as well, each of these items you can drill into and see the history of.
So, you can see the floor plan history here, if you'd like.
And this will show asking rent versus net effective rent.
At a floor plan level.
And each unit, you can also drill into as well.
So if I come in to view one of these units that was leased, I can drill into that unit, and I can see exactly what the price point was for how long.
Looks like I got a test unit there, but, in our demo environment, but let me see if I can pull a real one here.
So, a lot of granularity in the reports.
Final thing on comps, one of the things that makes ApartmentIQ as popular as it is, we created this category of automated market surveys.
And so, we've got the most historical data.
So, you can go back, to October of 2020 and see just about anything you'd like to about the property itself.
And its comps.

So, you can, here is the, the chart that shows, the actual rents, which you can always toggle between median versus minimum rents, net effective rent per square foot, net effective rent per square foot.
You can look at available versus leased.
And then you can also see the specific comps behind this, what they're charging.

For any of this, so this is a property level right now, but you could always change this to any of the bed types that you want, and you're going to see this table below, which you can export, which shows all that historical data on a per comp basis.
You've got the average there, too, if you want to do a quick comparison of how the property's comparing to that.
Same thing for concessions.
You can see this below here.
Concessions is a percentage of rent.
You can see the specific comps themselves, or you can see how the subject property compares to the comp median, and the entirety of the blue line is the full comp range.
